coredata.dc:title: Visible light photochemical vapor generation using metal-free g-C3N4/CQDs composites as catalyst: Selective and ultrasensitive detection of mercury by ICP-MS
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jingrong
Tian, Yunfei
Wu, Xi
Hou, Xiandeng
coredata.dc:description: Photochemical vapor generation (PCVG) is a green and effective sampling technology for analytical atomic spectrometry, and in recent years it has been received continuous attention. In this work, visible light photochemical vapor generation was achieved with metal-free g-C3N4/CQDs (carbon nitride/carbon quantum dots) composites as a photocatalyst, and used for selective and ultrasensitive detection of total mercury by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The limit of detection can be as low as 8ngL−1, making it one of the most sensitive methods for mercury determination. The visible light photochemical vapor generation can also be coupled to other analytical atomic spectrometric techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible light
Photochemical vapor generation
Mercury
Carbon nitride
Carbon quantum dots
Inductively coupled plasma
Mass spectrometry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photochemical vapor generation of different elements
Mechanism of enhanced visible-light PCVG of Hg by g-C3N4/CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of preparation of metal-free g-C3N4/CQDs composites.
Schematic diagram of visible light photochemical vapor generation.
SEM (a, b) of pure g-C3N4 (a) and No.1g-C3N4/CQDs composites (b) and TEM (c, d) of pure g-C3N4 (c) and No. 1g-C3N4/CQDs composites (d), respectively.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites of varied doping amount.
Photoluminescence of pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites of varied doping amount. Excitation wavelength: a: 293nm; b: 372nm.

Survey XPS spectra of pure g-C3N4 and No. 2g-C3N4/CQDs composites (a), high resolution XPS spectra of pure g-C3N4 (b: N spectrum; c: C spectrum) and No. 2g-C3N4/CQDs composites (d: N spectrum; e: C spectrum).

(a) The visible light PCVG catalyzed by pure g-C3N4; (b) The UV PCVG catalyzed by pure g-C3N4; (c) The visible light PCVG catalyzed by No.2g-C3N4/CQDs composites; (d) The UV PCVG catalyzed by No.2g-C3N4/CQDs composites. Experimental condition: HCOOH, 15%(v/v); photocatalyst, 0.2mgmL−1; concentration of metal elements, Hg, 100ngmL−1; the others, 500ngmL−1.
The visible light PCVG of Hg under different conditions. Experimental condition: HCOOH, 15% (v/v); No.2 photocatalyst, 0.2mgmL−1; concentration of Hg, 100ngmL−1; and AFS detection.

The optimization of the visible light PCVG experimental conditions. (a) HCOOH, 10% (v/v); and Hg, 1ngmL−1; (b) HCOOH, 10% (v/v); Hg, 1ngmL−1; No.2 photocatalyst, 0.2mgmL−1; (c) No.2 photocatalyst, 0.2mgmL−1; Hg, 1ngmL−1; (d) HCOOH, 10% (v/v); No.2 photocatalyst, 0.2mgmL−1; and Hg, 1ngmL−1.
The visible light photochemical vapor generation of Hg, MeHg and EtHg. Experimental condition: HCOOH, 10% (v/v); No.2 photocatalyst, 0.1mgmL−1; Hg, 5ngmL−1; MeHg, 200ngmL−1; and EtHg, 200ngmL−1.
The mechanism of visible-light chemical vapor generation of Hg catalyzed by g-C3N4/CQDs composites.
The working parameters for the ICP-MS or the AFS instruments.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Used for PCVG-ICP-MS; PMT: photomultiplier tube.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the LOD with those by other atomic spectrometric methods.

head.ce:title: Visible light photochemical vapor generation using metal-free g-C3N4/CQDs composites as catalyst: Selective and ultrasensitive detection of mercury by ICP-MS

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, Sichuan, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photochemical vapor generation (PCVG) is a green and effective sampling technology for analytical atomic spectrometry, and in recent years it has been received continuous attention. In this work, visible light photochemical vapor generation was achieved with metal-free g-C3N4/CQDs (carbon nitride/carbon quantum dots) composites as a photocatalyst, and used for selective and ultrasensitive detection of total mercury by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The limit of detection can be as low as 8ngL−1, making it one of the most sensitive methods for mercury determination. The visible light photochemical vapor generation can also be coupled to other analytical atomic spectrometric techniques.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to more sensitively and selectively detect toxic metal elements, a suitable sampling introduction technology is very important in analytical atomic spectrometry. Previous work [1,2] finds photochemical vapor generation (PCVG) a generally green and effective sampling introduction method. However, most PCVG technologies are achieved by ultraviolet irradiation (UV) [3] and visible light is rarely reported for such applications. Although many elements such as As, Sb, Bi, Te, Sn, Pb and Cd [4], Se [5], Hg [6], and Fe, Co and Ni [7] can be photochemically vapor-generated using the UV as a light source, selective detection could be difficult.

Metal-free photocatalyst g-C3N4/CQDs (carbon nitride/carbon quantum dots) composites has caught a lot of attentions since Kang et al. [8] published their work on carbon nanodot-carbon nitride (C3N4) nanocomposites for stable visible light water splitting. It is found that g-C3N4/CQDs nanocomposites are stable, cost-effective, and easy to prepare. g-C3N4/CQDs nanocomposites has also been used for degradation of Rhodamine B under UV irradiation and photocatalytic hydrogen production [9]. However, there is no report about metal ion reduction using g-C3N4/CQDs composites as a photocatalyst. The band gap of earth-abundant and highly stable C3N4 is about 2.7eV [10], and its low quantum efficiency makes it difficult to make full use of visible-light for photocatalysis. Many measures have been taken to achieve better quantum efficiency, such as doping [11], composing with other materials [12] and morphology control [13]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [14] and C3N4 are the best partners. The doping of CQDs makes the composites high quantum efficiency and photocatalytic activities. CQDs can be the electron acceptor, which makes the effective photo-generated electrons and holes separation. Therefore, metal-free photocatalyst g-C3N4/CQDs composites may be a good photocatalyst for metal reduction in visible light photochemical vapor generation.

Mercury is one of the most toxic metals [15,16], and it is often studied as the metal pollution marker [17], partially since it widely exists in the environment [18], water, fish [19,20], and animals [21]. There are many PCVG methods for mercury vapor generation [22], but highly effective visible light PCVG of Hg vapor has not yet been studied by researchers. In this work, therefore, we mainly make use of metal-free g-C3N4/CQDs composites as the visible light photocatalyst to realize the selective PCVG of Hg for its subsequent detection by highly sensitive inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS).

18.2MΩ-cm deionized water was used in this experiment from a water purification system (Pure Technology Co., Ltd., Chengdu, China). The standard solutions of Hg, Ni, Fe, Sn, Cd, Pb, Se, Te, Zn, Cu, Ca, As, Bi and Sb were bought from the National Standard Material Center (Beijing, China). All reagents were at least of analytical grade. Melamine and formic acid were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). l-ascorbic acid and ethanol were from Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China).

An inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICAP Q ICP-MS) was from Thermo Fisher Scientific. An atomic fluorescence spectrometer (Model AFS-9600, Beijing Haiguang Instrument Co., Beijing, China) was also used in this work. X-ray diffraction (XRD, X'Pert Pro MPD, Philips) spectra of the materials were obtained to compared with those reported [9]. An F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a quartz cell (1cm×1cm) was used for measurement of fluorescence. A JSM-7500F scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, Japan) and a Tecnai G2F20 S-TWIN (FEI Co., USA) transmission electron microscope (TEM) were used for characterizing the morphology of the metal-free g-C3N4/CQDs composites. A UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to obtain the UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra for the dry samples. A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo Fishier Scientific) was used to record the FT-IR spectra from 4000 to 400cm−1. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD 800 X, Kratos, UK) was used in this work. Visible LED lamps (3W, Kiwi Fruit Photoelectric Company, Shenzhen, China) and ultraviolet lamps (15W, Philips Co., Holland) were used for irradiation during the PCVG in this work.

Firstly, 10g melamine was calcined at the temperature of 550°C for 4h. The resultant yellow agglomerate was milled into powder in a mortar, and the powder was heated to 500°C for 2h to make it to be converted to g-C3N4 nanosheet [23]. Then, different amounts (No.1: 0.55g; No.2: 0.27g; No.3: 0.14g; No. 4:1.1g) of l-ascorbic acid was added into the mixture of 12.5mL H2O and 12.5mL ethanol. In addition, 0.5g g-C3N4 nanosheets was added, as shown in Fig. 1 . The above mixtures were heated to 180°C in high pressure and high temperature reactors for 4h. After centrifugation, the g-C3N4/CQDs composites (named as No.1, No.2, No.3 and No.4, respectively) were harvested and dried overnight at 100°C in vacuum.

Solutions of 0.1mgmL−1 g-C3N4/CQDs composites and different concentrations of Hg were prepared in 10mL 2% (v/v) formic acid medium. The solution was pumped into the quartz coil and then irradiated for 30s. After the irradiation, 10 LEDs were turned off, the gas valve was switched, and the reaction solution was immediately carried into the gas liquid separator (GLS) by carried gas Ar, where the mercury vapor was immediately swept into the detector (ICP-MS or AFS) for detection, as shown Fig. 2 . The instrumental parameters for the ICP-MS or the AFS are summarized in Table 1 .

It was found that two diffraction peaks existed in the XRD patterns of pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites. The g-C3N4 showed the peaks of 12.8° and 27.6°. However, the g-C3N4/CQDs composites showed the peaks of 13° and 27.6°. The results were consistent with those in the literature [9]. The 13° represented the plane packing motif of tri-s-triazine units, which was indexed as (100) peak. And the 27.6° was indexed as (002) peak which showed the stack of aromatic structure. With the addition of CQDs, the subtle change in the XRD peak is probably because the introduction of CQDs destroys the intact structure of g-C3N4 and thus causes the move of the peak.

The morphology of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites was shown in Fig. 3 . The SEM and TEM photos showed the different feature after introduction of CQDs. From the SEM, no obvious change can be observed. The g-C3N4 was piled up, but obvious interspace can be observed between the nanosheets (Fig. 3b). The nanosheet structure can be clearly distinguished by the TEM. As in Fig. 3d, the CQDs below 5nm were among the g-C3N4 nanosheets, i.e., the CQDs and the g-C3N4 were well recombined.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were compared of pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites of varied doping amount, as shown in Fig. 4 . When appropriate amount of CQDs were combined with g-C3N4, their absorbance were higher than the pure g-C3N4. It can be seen that No. 2g-C3N4/CQDs photocatalyst had the highest absorbance, which was thought to have the best light harvesting ability, a very important factor in the photocatalytic activities.

The FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites showed that just few changes occurred after doping of CQDs. Vibrations at 1630 (CN), 1570 (CN), 1400 (CN), 1315 (CN) and 1250cm−1(CN) show the typical CN chemical bonds, 881cm−1 is the triazine structure, and a broad and large vibration at 3200cm−1 represents the NH bond.

The photoluminescence (PL) showed the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes. Therefore, the intensity of PL accounted for the separation ability of photo-generated electrons and holes, which determined the photocatalytic activity. Fig. 5a and b exhibited the different photoluminescence excited by 293nm and 372nm, respectively. The results showed that No.3 and No.2g-C3N4/CQDs catalysts had the lowest photoluminescence, which represented the less recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes and had better photocatalytic activity. The photoluminescence reduced after doping of CQDs and the composites were thus more beneficial to photocatalysis.

Pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites (No.2 catalyst) were characterized by XPS, as shown in Fig. 6 . The survey spectra (Fig. 6a) exhibit that pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites were made up of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. From the high resolution XPS spectra of N (Fig. 6b and d), the N species did not change so much after doping of CQDs. Referring to the literatures [24–26] and deducing by the molecular structure, we give the fitting results of C and N spectra. In the C XPS spectra, pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQDs composites had the graphitic C (284.5), CN (285.3), CO (286.2) and NCN (287.8) [27]. And in the N spectra, three N species, CNC (398.3), NC (399.5) and N(C)3 (400.5) can be distinguished. Compared with pure g-C3N4, graphitic C (284.5), CN (285.3) and CO (286.2) had the increased peak intensities relative to NCN (287.8) in g-C3N4/CQDs composites, illustrating the inclusion of CQDs [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photochemical vapor generation of different elements
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the PCVG activity of g-C3N4/CQDs composites, ions of 14 elements were determined by PCVG-ICP-MS with results shown in Fig. 7 . It is very clear that g-C3N4/CQDs composites have better photocatalytic activities than pure g-C3N4. The results illustrates that the doping of CQDs makes better light absorption and better separation of photo-generated electrons and holes. In the photocatalysis with g-C3N4/CQDs composites, the UV light has better photocatalytic activities than the visible light. Among the selected 14 elements, Hg, Se, Pb, As, Co, Ni, Cu, Sn, Sb, Bi and Te can be photochemical vapor-generated by the UV. However, Cd, Fe and Zn can hardly be photochemical vapor-generated regardless the catalyst. Pure g-C3N4 shows somewhat different photocatalytic activities towards these elements, and it has preferable photocatalytic activities towards Cu, Se, Ni, Co, Bi, Hg and As. In the visible light photocatalysis of pure g-C3N4, Hg, Se and Co can be more efficiently photochemical vapor-generated. However, in the visible light photocatalysis of g-C3N4/CQDs composites, the PCVG efficiency of Hg is obviously far much higher than those of other elements. Therefore, Hg can be selectively chemical vapor generated by visible light.

The visible light PCVG of Hg was proceeded and compared under different conditions, with results shown in Fig. 8 . It is demonstrated that the LED visible-light and g-C3N4/CQDs composites enhanced the PCVG efficiency of Hg.

The quantity of the photocatalyst, illumination time, gas flow rate, and acid concentration were optimized, with results shown in Fig. 9 . It is showed that 0.1mgmL−1 No.2 photocatalyst and 2% (v/v) formic acid under 30s illumination time can make the best visible light PCVG of Hg. That No.2 photocatalyst made the best vapor generation accorded with the characterization results of the photocatalyst. The best gas flow rate was found to be 700mLmin−1.

Under the optimized conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) for Hg was found to be as low as 8ngL−1, which was comparable with those by other analytical atomic spectrometric methods (Table 2 ). The linear range was from 0.01ngmL−1 to 100ngmL−1 with regression coefficients (R2) greater than 0.993. The sensitivity of this visible light PCVG was 47 times higher than traditional nebulization method in terms of the ratio of slopes of the calibration curves. The relative standard deviation (RSD, n=10) of 1ngmL−1 Hg was 4.1%.

Furthermore, the visible light PCVG of MeHg and EtHg was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 10 . Both MeHg and EtHg can be photochemical vapor-generated, but obviously with much lower efficiency compared to that of Hg. The differentiation in PCVG efficiency could be used for mercury speciation analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of enhanced visible-light PCVG of Hg by g-C3N4/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 11 , g-C3N4 is a semiconductor with a band gap of about 2.7eV, and it has been used as a visible-light photocatalyst. When it is doped with CQDs, the resultant g-C3N4/CQDs composites have better light absorbance capability. The results of the photoluminescence experiments also accounted for the promoted separation efficiency of photo-generated electrons and holes of the doped nanomaterials, although too much CQDs led themselves to be recombination centers. Here the generated electrons were used for reduction of Hg2+ to Hg0 [29,30]. At the same time, HCOOH consumed the holes, and was oxidized to CO2, as schematically shown in Fig. 11 [5,30].

Metal-free g-C3N4/CQDs composites were facilely synthesized, carefully characterized and successfully used as a photocatalyst for visible light selective PCVG of mercury vapor. The PCVG was coupled to ICP-MS for ultrasensitive determination of Hg, with an impressive LOD of Hg comparable to existent methods. In addition, MeHg and EtHg also can be visible-light chemical vapor-generated, but with very low efficiency, and this could be used for mercury speciation analysis in the future. Finally, this new visible light PCVG can also be coupled to other analytical atomic spectrometric techniques.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21529501). We also thank our Analytical & Testing Center for the characterization of the materials.
